speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling off since yesterday—kind of achy and tired.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let me start by checking your temperature—it’s 37.9 °C, which is slightly elevated.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve had a headache all day—dull but persistent, mostly across my forehead.

speaker: doctor
text: Any nausea, visual changes, or neck stiffness with that headache? And have you had fevers or chills?

speaker: patient
text: No nausea or stiffness, but I did feel warm earlier—I thought maybe I was coming down with something.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s listen to your heart and lungs first. You mentioned chest discomfort before—any this week?

speaker: patient
text: Just mild pressure when walking up stairs—not like before, but it’s there.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin is 163 g/L—well within normal range, so no anemia concern right now.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure is 69 mmHg—on the lower side, but not alarming yet.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your history, stress test results, and current symptoms, I’m confirming coronary artery disease as the primary diagnosis.

speaker: doctor
text: I just rechecked your temperature—it’s now 36.5 °C, so the earlier elevation may have been transient.

speaker: patient
text: That makes sense—I drank two glasses of water and rested before coming in.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll also monitor your blood work closely. Your lipid panel looks stable, and renal function is normal.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been taking my atenolol every morning, but sometimes I skip it if I feel lightheaded.

speaker: doctor
text: Skipping doses can be risky—especially with CAD. Let’s adjust your regimen to improve consistency.

speaker: doctor
text: Interestingly, your repeat hemoglobin came back at 176 g/L—higher than the earlier 163 g/L. Could reflect hydration status or lab variation, but we’ll track it.

speaker: patient
text: I noticed my thermometer at home read 37.9 °C again this morning—same as that first reading you took.

speaker: patient
text: That reading matches the one you got when I first walked in—the 37.9 °C.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m going to switch you from atenolol to bisoprolol—lower dose, more consistent absorption, and less likely to cause lightheadedness.

speaker: doctor
text: One last check: your diastolic pressure is now 82 mmHg—but you mentioned earlier it was 69 mmHg, and that’s the number we saw at the start.
